title: Ensure AWS Config configuration changes are monitored

input_row.title: Ensure AWS Config configuration changes are monitored
input_row.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for detecting changes to AWS Config's configurations.
input_row.rationale: CloudWatch is an AWS native service that allows you to observe and monitor resources and applications. CloudTrail logs can also be sent to an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment for monitoring and alerting. Monitoring changes to the AWS Config configuration will help ensure sustained visibility of the configuration items within the AWS account.
input_row.audit: If you are using CloudTrail trails and CloudWatch, perform the following to ensure that there is at least one active multi-region CloudTrail trail with the prescribed metric filters and alarms configured: 1. Identify the log group name that is configured for use with the active multi-region CloudTrail trail: • List all CloudTrail trails: aws cloudtrail describe-trails • Identify multi-region CloudTrail trails: Trails with "IsMultiRegionTrail" set to true • Note the value associated with "Name":<trail-name> • Note the <trail-log-group-name> within the value associated with "CloudWatchLogsLogGroupArn" o Example: arn:aws:logs:<region>:<account-id>:log- group:<trail-log-group-name>:* • Ensure the identified multi-region CloudTrail trail is active: o aws cloudtrail get-trail-status --name <trail-name> § Ensure IsLogging is set to TRUE • Ensure the identified multi-region CloudTrail trail captures all management events: o aws cloudtrail get-event-selectors --trail-name <trail-name> § Ensure there is at least one event selector for a trail with IncludeManagementEvents set to true and ReadWriteType set to All 2. Get a list of all associated metric filters for the <trail-log-group-name> captured in step 1: 3. aws logs describe-metric-filters --log-group-name <trail-log-group-name> 4. Ensure the output from the above command contains the following: 5. "filterPattern": "{ ($.eventSource = config.amazonaws.com) && (($.eventName=StopConfigurationRecorder)||($.eventName=DeleteDeliveryChannel)||($.eventName=PutDeliveryChannel)||($.eventName=PutConfigurationRecorder)) }" 6. Note the <aws-config-changes-metric> value associated with the filterPattern from step 3. 7. Get a list of CloudWatch alarms, and filter on the <aws-config-changes-metric> captured in step 4: 8. aws cloudwatch describe-alarms --query 'MetricAlarms[?MetricName==<aws-config-changes-metric>]' 9. Note the AlarmActions value; this will provide the SNS topic ARN value. 10. Ensure there is at least one active subscriber to the SNS topic: 11. aws sns list-subscriptions-by-topic --topic-arn <sns-topic-arn> • At least one subscription should have "SubscriptionArn" with a valid AWS ARN. o Example of valid "SubscriptionArn": arn:aws:sns:<region>:<account- id>:<sns-topic-name>:<subscription-id>
input_row.remediation: If you are using CloudTrail trails and CloudWatch, perform the following steps to set up the metric filter, alarm, SNS topic, and subscription: 1. Create a metric filter based on the provided filter pattern that checks for AWS Configuration changes and uses the <trail-log-group-name> taken from audit step 1: 2. aws logs put-metric-filter --log-group-name <trail-log-group-name> --filter-name <aws-config-changes-metric> --metric-transformations metricName=<aws-config-changes-metric>,metricNamespace='CISBenchmark',metricValue=1 --filter-pattern '{ ($.eventSource = config.amazonaws.com) && (($.eventName=StopConfigurationRecorder)||($.eventName=DeleteDeliveryChannel)||($.eventName=PutDeliveryChannel)||($.eventName=PutConfigurationRecorder)) }' Note: You can choose your own metricName and metricNamespace strings. Using the same metricNamespace for all Foundations Benchmark metrics will group them together. 3. Create an SNS topic that the alarm will notify: 4. aws sns create-topic --name <sns-topic-name> Note: You can execute this command once and then reuse the same topic for all monitoring alarms. Note: Capture the TopicArn that is displayed when creating the SNS topic in step 2. 5. Create an SNS subscription for the topic created in step 2: 6. aws sns subscribe --topic-arn <sns-topic-arn> --protocol <sns-protocol> --notification-endpoint <sns-subscription-endpoints> Note: You can execute this command once and then reuse the same subscription for all monitoring alarms. 7. Create an alarm that is associated with the CloudWatch Logs metric filter created in step 1 and the SNS topic created in step 2: 8. aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm --alarm-name <aws-config-changes-alarm> --metric-name <aws-config-changes-metric> --statistic Sum --period 300 --threshold 1 --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold --evaluation-periods 1 --namespace 'CISBenchmark' --alarm-actions <sns-topic-arn>
input_row.default_value: By default, CloudTrail logs AWS Config events (e.g., StopConfigurationRecorder, DeleteDeliveryChannel), but no CloudWatch metric filters or alarms exist. These changes are captured but not actively monitored unless configured.
input_row.additional_information: Configuring a log metric filter and alarm on a multi-region (global) CloudTrail trail: • ensures that activities from all regions (both used and unused) are monitored • ensures that activities on all supported global services are monitored • ensures that all management events across all regions are monitored

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_cloudtrail_multi_region_monitoring_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. **CloudTrail Trails**: Use `aws cloudtrail describe-trails` to list all CloudTrail trails in the account.
   - Validate that there is at least one multi-region trail by checking for `"IsMultiRegionTrail": true`.
   - Ensure the trail is active by using `aws cloudtrail get-trail-status --name <trail-name>` and confirming that `IsLogging` is TRUE.

2. **Event Selectors**: Use `aws cloudtrail get-event-selectors --trail-name <trail-name>` to verify that the trail captures all management events.
   - Check that there is at least one event selector with `IncludeManagementEvents` set to true and `ReadWriteType` set to `All`.

3. **Metric Filters**: Use `aws logs describe-metric-filters --log-group-name <trail-log-group-name>` to retrieve the associated metric filters for the identified log group.
   - Confirm that the output contains the specified filter pattern for AWS Config changes:
     ```json
     {
       "$.eventSource = config.amazonaws.com &&
       ($.eventName=StopConfigurationRecorder ||
       $.eventName=DeleteDeliveryChannel ||
       $.eventName=PutDeliveryChannel ||
       $.eventName=PutConfigurationRecorder)"
     }
     ```

4. **CloudWatch Alarms**: Use `aws cloudwatch describe-alarms --query 'MetricAlarms[?MetricName==<aws-config-changes-metric>]'` to check for existing alarms linked to the specified metric.
   - Ensure `AlarmActions` includes the SNS topic ARN.

5. **SNS Subscriptions**: Use `aws sns list-subscriptions-by-topic --topic-arn <sns-topic-arn>` to confirm that there is at least one active subscriber to the SNS topic.
   - Verify that at least one subscription has a valid `SubscriptionArn`.

**Pass/Fail Criteria**:
- The program checks that:
  - There is at least one multi-region active CloudTrail.
  - It captures all management events.
  - There are metric filters established for AWS Config changes.
  - There is an active CloudWatch alarm set for the metric.
  - There is at least one subscription to the SNS topic linked to the CloudWatch alarm.
  
If all criteria are met, the control passes; if any criteria fail, the control does not meet compliance.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification as "Automated" is correct. All the checks can be performed using AWS APIs, specifically through AWS CLI commands or boto3 methods. The use of AWS CloudTrail, CloudWatch, and SNS can be programmatically verified, which aligns with the automated approach.
- AWS SDK capabilities and AWS CLI commands are appropriately leveraged for this control.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The use of `aws cloudtrail describe-trails`, `aws cloudtrail get-trail-status`, `aws cloudtrail get-event-selectors`, `aws logs describe-metric-filters`, `aws cloudwatch describe-alarms`, and `aws sns list-subscriptions-by-topic` are valid and appropriate for the checks described.
- The JSON filter pattern for AWS Config changes is correctly specified.
- The program name `aws_cloudtrail_multi_region_monitoring_configured` is somewhat descriptive but could be improved for clarity.
- The description of the checks is clear and follows a logical sequence, ensuring that all necessary components are verified.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** The current program name could be more descriptive. A suggestion would be `aws_cloudtrail_config_changes_monitoring` to better reflect the specific focus on AWS Config changes.
- **Additional Checks:** Consider verifying that the SNS topic is configured with appropriate permissions to ensure that alarms can trigger notifications. This can be done using `aws sns get-topic-attributes`.
- **Event Selector Verification:** Ensure that the event selector also includes data events if necessary, depending on the organization's policy. This might require additional verification.
- **CloudWatch Alarm Verification:** It might be beneficial to check the alarm state to ensure it is set to `OK` or `ALARM` and not `INSUFFICIENT_DATA`.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is thorough and uses appropriate AWS services and commands. The technical details are mostly accurate, and the automation approach is well-justified.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Update the program name to `aws_cloudtrail_config_changes_monitoring` for better clarity.
- **Enhance Checks:** Add verification for SNS topic permissions and consider checking the alarm state.
- Overall, the assessment is solid, but these minor improvements can enhance its robustness and clarity.
